verdicts.reasoning: The scanner flagged preg_replace('/[^\+\d]/', '', $sms_phone) as an RCE sink because escapeshellarg() was not used. This is a false positive on multiple levels: (1) preg_replace without the /e modifier does NOT execute code -- it simply performs string replacement; (2) the result is stored in order/user meta via update_order_meta/update_user_meta, never passed to shell execution; (3) the regex itself is a whitelist filter that only allows + and digits, removing all other characters. File: includes/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-sms-consent.php:426

Same pattern as rce-001. preg_replace('/[^\+\d]/', '', $sms_phone) without /e modifier is not an RCE vector. The value is sanitized via sanitize_text_field() first, then whitelist-filtered to only digits and +, then stored in order/user meta. No shell execution anywhere in the flow. File: includes/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-newsletter.php:486

Scanner flagged preg_replace('/[^A-Za-z0-9\-\@\.]/', '', $_POST['data']['username']) as RCE because escapeshellarg() was not used. This is not an RCE sink -- preg_replace without /e is safe string replacement. The result is appended to a hardcoded URL for wp_remote_get(). The function mailchimp_woocommerce_ajax_create_account_signup() also calls $this->adminOnlyMiddleware() which requires current_user_can(mailchimp_get_allowed_capability()). File: admin/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-admin.php:1459

unserialize($cart->cart) at service.php:893 operates on data from the mailchimp_carts database table. The mc_cart_id from $_GET is used as a lookup key via $wpdb->prepare() (parameterized query, no SQLi). The cart column data is written only by trackCart() using maybe_serialize($this->cart) where $this->cart comes from WC()->cart->get_cart() -- a WooCommerce internal cart session, not user-controlled input. An attacker cannot inject arbitrary serialized payloads into this table without a separate SQL injection or database access. The handleCampaignTracking() function runs on init (no auth required), but the data source is trusted. File: includes/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-service.php:893

unserialize($job_row->job) at service.php:1409 operates on data from the mailchimp_jobs database table. The $obj_id parameter comes from WordPress Action Scheduler (not user input), and the SQL query uses $wpdb->prepare(). The job column contains serialized job objects written by the plugin's own job queue system. No user-controlled path to inject arbitrary serialized data. File: includes/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-service.php:1409

The wp_remote_post() call at tower.php:905 uses a URL that is constructed server-side from stored options/configuration. The $post_url is not derived from user input -- it's a hardcoded Mailchimp tower endpoint. The payload body contains server-side values (store_name from options, domain from get_option('siteurl'), etc.). No user-controllable URL parameter. File: includes/api/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-tower.php:905

The function mailchimp_woocommerce_ajax_oauth_status() calls $this->adminOnlyMiddleware() at line 1150, which checks current_user_can(mailchimp_get_allowed_capability()) and dies with wp_send_json_error if unauthorized. Only authenticated admins can reach this endpoint. The wp_send_json_error/success functions output JSON with Content-Type: application/json header, which browsers do not render as HTML, so XSS is not possible even if the response contains unsanitized data. File: admin/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-admin.php:1164
Same as xss-001. Admin-only AJAX handler, JSON response with application/json content type. wp_send_json_success outputs JSON, not HTML. File: admin/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-admin.php:1168
Same as xss-001. Admin-only AJAX handler, JSON response. File: admin/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-admin.php:1170

The wp_remote_post() call at line 1190 uses a hardcoded URL 'https://woocommerce.mailchimpapp.com/api/finish'. The user-supplied $_POST['token'] is included only in the JSON body payload, not in the URL. This is not SSRF -- the destination URL is fixed and cannot be manipulated by the attacker. The function also requires admin auth via adminOnlyMiddleware(). File: admin/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-admin.php:1190
Admin-only AJAX handler (adminOnlyMiddleware), JSON response via wp_send_json_error. JSON content type prevents XSS. File: admin/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-admin.php:1195
Admin-only AJAX handler (adminOnlyMiddleware), JSON response via wp_send_json_success. JSON content type prevents XSS. File: admin/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-admin.php:1249
Admin-only AJAX handler (adminOnlyMiddleware), JSON response via wp_send_json_error. JSON content type prevents XSS. File: admin/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-admin.php:1252

The wp_remote_get() at line 1262 uses a hardcoded base URL 'https://woocommerce.mailchimpapp.com/api/usernames/available/' with the username appended as a path segment. sanitize_text_field() strips tags and extra whitespace but does not prevent path manipulation. However, the URL scheme/host is hardcoded and cannot be changed. The username is appended as a path component to a fixed Mailchimp API endpoint -- the worst case is the admin checking a different username path, which is not exploitable SSRF. Also requires admin auth. File: admin/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-admin.php:1262
Admin-only AJAX handler (adminOnlyMiddleware at line 1259), JSON response. File: admin/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-admin.php:1265
Admin-only AJAX handler (adminOnlyMiddleware), JSON response via wp_send_json_success. File: admin/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-admin.php:1269

Same as ssrf-003. Hardcoded base URL 'https://woocommerce.mailchimpapp.com/api/usernames/suggestions/' with username filtered by preg_replace('/[^A-Za-z0-9\-\@\.]/', '', $username). Admin-only. Not SSRF. File: admin/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-admin.php:1277
Admin-only AJAX handler, JSON response. File: admin/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-admin.php:1280
Admin-only AJAX handler, JSON response. File: admin/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-admin.php:1283

The source is get_option('admin_email') which is a WordPress core option set by the site administrator, not user input. The URL for wp_remote_post is hardcoded to 'https://woocommerce.mailchimpapp.com/api/support'. Not SSRF. File: admin/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-admin.php:1372

Source is get_option('admin_email'), a trusted WordPress option. The function mailchimp_send_sync_finished_email() is called internally, not via user-facing endpoints. The wp_send_json_error output is JSON content type. File: admin/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-admin.php:1375

Same as ssrf-004. Hardcoded base URL with username appended. The function mailchimp_woocommerce_ajax_create_account_signup() calls adminOnlyMiddleware(). Not SSRF. File: admin/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-admin.php:1460
Admin-only AJAX handler (adminOnlyMiddleware at line 1404), JSON response via wp_send_json_error. File: admin/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-admin.php:1463

The function mailchimp_woocommerce_tower_status() calls $this->adminOnlyMiddleware() at line 2456. JSON response via wp_send_json_error. $original_opt comes from $this->getData() (internal DB), not user input reflected back. File: admin/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-admin.php:2476

The function mailchimp_woocommerce_ajax_delete_log_file() calls adminOnlyMiddleware() at line 2543. The $_POST['log_file'] value is passed to WC_Log_Handler_File::remove() which is a WooCommerce core method that validates the filename internally. The response is wp_send_json_success(['success' => $removed]) where $removed is a boolean. No XSS possible. Note: path traversal through WC_Log_Handler_File::remove() is mitigated by WooCommerce's own filename validation. File: admin/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-admin.php:2554

The function mailchimp_woocommerce_send_event() calls adminOnlyMiddleware() at line 2594. The $mc_event value is used as an array key lookup against a hardcoded $map. If the key exists, it calls Mailchimp_Woocommerce_Event::track() with the mapped (hardcoded) value. If not, $payload = 'nothing'. The response is JSON. No user input is reflected unsanitized. File: admin/class-mailchimp-woocommerce-admin.php:2614

The source $selected_timezone comes from stored plugin options or get_option('timezone_string') -- both are admin-set values, not user input. The output uses esc_attr() and esc_html() for the $t['zone'] values, and $selected_timezone is only used in a comparison ($t['zone'] === $selected_timezone) for the selected() function, never echoed directly. This is an admin-only template. File: admin/v2/templates/connect-accounts/create-account-page.php:232
Same as xss-016. Admin template, stored options as source, proper escaping with esc_attr/esc_html. File: admin/v2/templates/connect-accounts/create-account-popup.php:139

The source is get_option('blogname'), which is set by the WordPress administrator. This is an admin-only template (connect-accounts/header.php). While the blogname is not escaped with esc_html() before being concatenated with HTML, the value can only be set by an admin -- so this would require admin-level access to exploit (self-XSS). The template is only rendered on the plugin's admin settings page which requires admin access to view. File: admin/v2/templates/connect-accounts/header.php:13

The source $_GET['tab'] is sanitized with both sanitize_key() (which lowercases and strips non-alphanumeric characters except dashes and underscores) and esc_attr() before being stored in $active_tab. When echoed at line 41, the value has already been stripped of all XSS-relevant characters by sanitize_key(). This is also an admin-only template. File: admin/v2/templates/confirmation/content.php:41

The source is mailchimp_get_option('admin_email') or get_option('admin_email'), both admin-set values. The printf format string uses esc_html__() which escapes the template. The $admin_email is inserted via %s in the already-escaped template string. While the email itself is not separately escaped, it can only be set by an admin. This is an admin-only template. File: admin/v2/templates/confirmation/header.php:33

The output uses esc_url() around the entire URL construction, sanitize_title() on $viewed_log, and wp_nonce_url for CSRF protection. esc_url() properly sanitizes URLs for HTML output. This is properly escaped. File: admin/v2/templates/confirmation/tabs/logs.php:117

Source is get_option('woocommerce_checkout_page_id'), which is a WooCommerce core option (page ID integer). get_the_permalink() converts it to a URL. The echo sprintf() outputs HTML with a link. While the permalink is not escaped with esc_url(), the page ID is set by WooCommerce installation and the permalink is generated by WordPress core. This is an admin-only template. File: admin/v2/templates/confirmation/tabs/store-info.php:106
Same as xss-020. Admin email from options, esc_html__() template, admin-only page. File: admin/v2/templates/confirmation/tabs/advanced.php:51
Output uses esc_url() and date_i18n() with get_option() values. date_i18n output is safe (formatted date string). Admin-only template. File: admin/partials/tabs/logs.php:80
Same as xss-021. Properly escaped with esc_url(), sanitize_title(), wp_nonce_url. Admin-only template. File: admin/partials/tabs/logs.php:93

Source is $options['campaign_from_name'] or get_option('blogname') -- both admin-set stored values. While echoed without esc_html() in an input field value context, this is an admin-only settings page. An admin setting their own blogname to a malicious value is self-XSS, not a vulnerability. File: admin/partials/tabs/campaign_defaults.php:26
Same as xss-026. Source is $options['campaign_from_email'] or get_option('admin_email'). Admin-only settings page, admin-set values. File: admin/partials/tabs/campaign_defaults.php:33
Same pattern. Source is $options['campaign_permission_reminder'] or blogname. Admin-set values on admin-only page. File: admin/partials/tabs/campaign_defaults.php:68
Source is $options['store_name'] or get_option('blogname'). Admin-set values on admin-only settings page. File: admin/partials/tabs/store_info.php:21
Source is $options['admin_email'] or get_option('admin_email'). Admin-set values on admin-only settings page. File: admin/partials/tabs/store_info.php:28
Same as xss-016. Timezone from stored options, proper esc_attr/esc_html escaping on output, admin-only template. File: admin/partials/tabs/store_info.php:134

The source is get_user_meta() for the subscription status. The output uses a ternary that only outputs either 'archived' or '1' as literal strings: ($mailchimp_user_subscription_status === 'archived' ? 'archived' : '1'). The user meta value is never directly reflected -- only the comparison result (a hardcoded string) is output. No XSS possible. File: public/partials/mailchimp-woocommerce-my-account.php:41

summary: All 43 automated findings are false positives. The scanner incorrectly flagged: (1) preg_replace without /e modifier as RCE sinks; (2) unserialize on plugin-controlled database data as object injection when no user-controlled path to inject serialized payloads exists; (3) wp_remote_post/get with hardcoded URLs as SSRF; (4) admin-only AJAX handlers returning JSON as XSS when wp_send_json_* sets Content-Type: application/json; (5) admin-only templates echoing admin-set options (blogname, admin_email) as XSS. The plugin has proper auth checks via adminOnlyMiddleware() on all admin AJAX handlers, uses parameterized queries for DB access, and the nopriv AJAX endpoints (set_user_by_email, get_user_by_hash) do not expose dangerous functionality.
feedback: Scanner has extremely high false positive rate on this plugin (100%). Key issues: (1) preg_replace without /e is NOT an RCE sink -- the /e modifier was deprecated in PHP 5.5 and removed in PHP 7.0; (2) unserialize of database-stored data is only dangerous when the attacker can control what's stored, which requires tracing the write path, not just the read path; (3) wp_send_json_* functions set Content-Type: application/json which prevents XSS -- scanner should recognize this pattern; (4) hardcoded URLs with user-controlled path segments appended are not SSRF; (5) admin-only templates should not be flagged unless there's a path traversal to reach them without admin auth.
